metadata.title: Five-Page Desktop Wireframes in Figma
metadata.playlist: Make It Visible — Visual Identity & Wireframes
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Assignment 7 (Visual) brief — 5 desktop wireframes in Figma; due 2026-07-17

beats.narration_text: Hola — Liam, Episode 4 of 6. The visual system is built. Now we build the structure. Wireframes are the floor plan of the website — they show which pages exist, what content is on each page, and how a user moves through it. They are not mockups. They do not use your brand colors. They use gray, white, and black boxes with labels. A wireframe done well is a blueprint any developer or AI site builder can follow without interpretation. This episode covers the five required pages, what to put on each, and how to build them in Figma.

props.command: I have my style guide. Generate wireframe specs for 5 desktop pages — Home, About, Features, Pricing, Contact — so I can build them in Figma.
props.runningText: wireframes = gray boxes + labels, no brand colors · 5 pages at 1440px · nav + footer on all…
output: 5 required pages: Home · About/Team · Product/Features · Pricing/Plans · Contact
Page anatomy: nav (logo + links + CTA) · content body · footer — same on all 5
Figma workflow: 1440px frames, named sections, exported as PNG

beats.act: what a wireframe is — not a mockup
beats.narration_text: Wireframes are structural documents, not design documents. The distinction matters because students often start adding color, brand fonts, and actual photography the moment they open Figma. Do not. Wireframes use three elements: rectangles for sections and images, text labels for content blocks and navigation items, and gray fills for visual hierarchy. Nothing else. The gray-box convention exists so the wireframe communicates structure without triggering aesthetic reactions. If you put your brand colors in the wireframe, reviewers will respond to the colors, not the structure. Keep it gray until the wireframe phase is done. The wireframe answers one question per page: what is the user supposed to do on this page? Every section on the page should serve that primary action. If a section does not contribute to the primary action, question whether it belongs on this page.

props.artifactHeading: Gray boxes. Text labels. One question per page.
artifactLines: 3 elements only: rectangles (sections/images) · text labels · gray fills.
No brand colors, no photography, no actual fonts. Not yet.
Gray-box convention: reviewers respond to structure, not aesthetics.
One question per page: what is the user supposed to DO on this page?
Every section should serve that primary action. If it doesn't, cut it.
Wireframe = structure. Mockup = design. Build structure first.
props.sparkLine: Add brand colors to a wireframe and reviewers respond to aesthetics, not structure.

beats.narration_text: Five required pages. Page one: Home. Hero section with headline and primary CTA, three to four feature highlights, social proof or testimonials block, secondary CTA in the footer area. Page two: About or Team. Origin story section, team or founder profiles, mission statement. Page three: Product or Features. Feature list with supporting visuals or icons, how-it-works section in three to five steps, a second CTA. Page four: Pricing or Plans. Plan comparison table, feature checklist per tier, a CTA under each tier. Page five: Contact. Contact form, location or time-zone context if relevant, alternative contact methods. Each page needs a nav at the top and a footer at the bottom — the nav and footer are the same across all five pages.

props.artifactHeading: Same nav + footer on all 5. Primary action per page.
artifactLines: Home: hero (headline + CTA) · 3–4 feature highlights · social proof · secondary CTA.
About/Team: origin story · team profiles · mission statement.
Product/Features: feature list · how-it-works (3–5 steps) · CTA.
Pricing/Plans: plan comparison table · feature checklist per tier · CTA per tier.
Contact: contact form · location/timezone context · alternative methods.
All 5 pages: same nav (logo + links + CTA) and same footer.
props.sparkLine: Nav and footer are the same across all 5 pages — build them once, reuse them.

beats.narration_text: Page anatomy for every page. Three sections every page shares: navigation at top, content body in the middle, footer at the bottom. The navigation contains the logo mark, the five page links, and the primary CTA button — for most student products this is 'Try it free' or 'Sign up.' The footer contains the logo, copyright line, and secondary links. The hero section on the Home page is the most important section to get right — it contains the headline, which says what the product does in six to ten words; the sub-headline, which says who it is for and why it matters in one to two sentences; and the primary CTA button. In the wireframe, the hero is represented as a full-width rectangle with three text labels: headline, sub-headline, and CTA button. That is the entire hero. No photography in the wireframe — a gray rectangle labeled 'hero image' if needed.

props.artifactHeading: Nav + body + footer. Hero = 3 text labels, nothing else.
artifactLines: Nav: logo mark · 5 page links · primary CTA button ('Try it free' / 'Sign up').
Footer: logo · copyright · secondary links.
Home hero: headline (6–10 words) · sub-headline (1–2 sentences) · CTA button.
In wireframe: hero = full-width rectangle with 3 labels. No photography.
Gray rectangle labeled 'hero image' if the section needs visual context.
Every section should contribute to the page's primary action or be cut.
props.sparkLine: The Home hero is 3 text labels in a rectangle. That's the wireframe. No photography.

beats.narration_text: Figma workflow for wireframes. Open a new Figma file. Create a frame for each page at 1440 pixels wide — the desktop standard. Name each frame with the page name: Home, About, Features, Pricing, Contact. Use Figma's rectangle tool for sections. Use the text tool for labels. Use the auto-layout feature to keep sections aligned and spaced consistently. Naming convention for layers: use the page name as the parent frame, section names as child frames within it. Export each page as a PNG at 1x. You will submit these five PNGs to Canvas along with the Figma board link. The grader will check structure: are the five pages there, does each page have a clear primary action, is there a nav and footer on all five? That is the wireframe rubric.

props.artifactHeading: 1440px frames · named sections · exported as PNG
artifactLines: New Figma file → 5 frames at 1440px wide. Name each: Home / About / Features / Pricing / Contact.
Tools: rectangle (sections/images) · text (labels) · auto-layout (alignment).
Layer naming: page name as parent frame, section name as child frame.
Export: each page as PNG at 1x.
Submit: 5 PNGs to Canvas + the Figma board link (set to 'anyone can view').
Grader checks: 5 pages present · primary action per page · nav + footer on all.
props.sparkLine: 5 frames. 5 names. Auto-layout. Export at 1x. Submit PNGs + Figma link.

beats.narration_text: Verdict. Five pages: Home, About, Features, Pricing, Contact. Three elements per wireframe: rectangles, text labels, gray fills — no color, no photography. Page anatomy: nav, content body, footer — same on all five pages. Figma workflow: 1440-pixel frames, one per page, named, exported as PNG. The wireframe is the brief a site builder acts on. If the wireframe is unclear, the site builder will make assumptions, and those assumptions will not match your brief. Five clear pages now means five predictable pages in A8.

props.artifactHeading: Gray boxes + labels = the brief a site builder acts on
artifactLines: 5 pages: Home · About · Features · Pricing · Contact.
3 elements: rectangles · text labels · gray fills. No color. No photography.
All 5 pages: same nav (logo + links + CTA) · same footer.
Figma: 1440px frames, one per page, named, exported as PNG at 1x.
Clear wireframes → predictable A8 output. Unclear wireframes → site builder guesses.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Here is the nina wireframe spec prompt. Paste it with your creative brief and style guide in context. The prompt reads: Here is my visual identity brief and style guide: [paste brief and style guide]. Generate wireframe specs for five desktop pages: Home, About or Team, Product or Features, Pricing or Plans, and Contact. For each page: list the sections in order, top to bottom. For each section, state the section name, the content labels inside it — headline, body text, CTA button, image placeholder — and the primary user action this section supports. Format for direct use in Figma: section names as I would label frames, content as placeholder labels not actual copy. Include the primary CTA for each page and the expected user path from this page to the next. After running the prompt: check that every page has a primary action. If a page has no clear primary action, the section list is not specific enough.

props.command: Here is my brief and style guide: [paste]. Generate wireframe specs for 5 desktop pages: Home, About/Team, Product/Features, Pricing/Plans, Contact. For each: list sections top-to-bottom with section names, content labels (headline/body/CTA/image placeholder), and the primary user action each section supports. Format for direct Figma use: section names = frame labels, content = placeholder labels not copy.
props.runningText: after the prompt: check that every page has a primary action — no primary action means the spec isn't specific enough…

beats.narration_text: Five-Page Desktop Wireframes in Figma. Liam, in for Bear.
